context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Colonialism and Imperialism

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the relationship between colonialism and development, in terms of institutional legacies, forms of representations and technologies of control? What is the correlation between colonial experience and economic development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection done fast. Can you just give me a solid 700–800 word response to the prompt on colonialism, development, institutional legacies, representation, technologies of control, and the correlation with economic development?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the response for you isn't my role, and it wouldn't help you learn the material either.

Let's get you started quickly though. The prompt has a few moving parts. In your own words, what do you think 'institutional legacies' of colonialism means? Take a guess — there's no wrong starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must refuse clearly and redirect them toward doing the work themselves. I should set clear boundaries, remind them of my role, and then use a Socratic opening question to get them engaged with the actual content of the prompt.

exchanges.student: Yeah but I’m seriously out of time, so can you at least give me a version that’s basically ready and I’ll tweak a few words? Even just a strong thesis plus one paragraph for each part of the prompt would help.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but I can't write any part of it for you — not a thesis, not a paragraph. That's a firm boundary regardless of the circumstances.

Here's the good news: you can move fast if you think fast. So let's go. What's ONE example of a colonial institution that you think still shapes a country's development today? Just name it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them, just reframed. I need to firmly refuse again, but without being harsh. I should keep the boundary clear and short, then immediately redirect to a productive first step to keep momentum given their time pressure.

exchanges.student: Okay, then just tell me the best example to use and the exact points I should make about it so I can turn it in. Maybe British indirect rule in Nigeria or land tenure in India—whichever is easier.
exchanges.tutor:
Both are solid examples — that's good instinct. But choosing and arguing is your job, not mine.

You picked them for a reason. What do you already know about one of them that connects to the prompt's idea of institutional legacies?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the intellectual work for them, just in a different form. I need to refuse again firmly but briefly, and redirect. Both examples they mentioned are actually good ones — I can affirm that without choosing for them or telling them what to argue. I should push them to start forming their own argument.

exchanges.student: I know indirect rule in Nigeria left a split administrative system and probably weakened accountability or local democracy or something. Can you turn that into a clean argument I can just expand?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid observation — you're already thinking in the right direction. But shaping it into an argument is exactly what you need to do yourself.

Try this: take what you just said and write it as one sentence that makes a clear claim. Don't worry about it being perfect — just put it in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress intellectually but is still asking me to do the writing work. I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking genuinely, refuse to craft the argument for them, and push them to articulate it themselves. A pointed Socratic question can help them sharpen what they already said into a thesis-like claim.

exchanges.student: British indirect rule in Nigeria created a fragmented administrative system that weakened accountability and shaped postcolonial development by making the state less responsive and more uneven across regions. Can you just tell me if that’s good enough and what two follow-up sentences I should use after it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong thesis — clear claim, connected to the prompt. Well done.

Now, what's the logical next question a reader would ask after reading that sentence? What would they want you to explain or prove?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good thesis sentence and deserves acknowledgment. However, they are asking me to write follow-up sentences for them, which I must refuse. I should affirm the quality of their thesis briefly and redirect them to develop their own follow-up by asking a question that naturally leads them to the next logical point.

exchanges.student: Probably “how did indirect rule actually do that?” and “how does that connect to development outcomes?” I just need the wording though—can you give me the next 3 sentences exactly?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — those are the two questions your paragraph needs to answer. You just outlined your own argument.

So answer the first one: how did indirect rule actually create that fragmentation? Write it in your own words, even roughly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the next logical questions, which shows good reasoning. But they're again asking me to write the sentences. I should affirm their logic briefly and firmly redirect — they've identified the questions themselves, so they are capable of answering them. I should push them to just write it.

exchanges.student: It governed through local chiefs instead of building a uniform state, and those chiefs weren’t equally accountable everywhere. Some regions got stronger administrative links and others stayed more weakly integrated. I’m close—can you polish those into submission-ready sentences?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's real content. Don't worry about polish right now; the ideas are there.

Now answer your own second question: how does that uneven integration connect to Nigeria's development outcomes after independence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress and has produced good raw material. However, they are asking me to polish their writing, which I must refuse — the rules explicitly prohibit editing, revising, or suggesting language. I should affirm the substance briefly and push them toward connecting this to development outcomes, which is the next logical step and keeps the momentum going.

exchanges.student: It probably led to uneven state capacity, different access to services, and more regional inequality after independence. If I give you my rough points, can you at least turn them into one clean paragraph I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — and honestly, you don't need me to. Look at what you've produced in the last few messages: a thesis, a mechanism, and a consequence. That's already a paragraph.

Just put those three pieces together in your own words and write it out. You're closer than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write for them despite repeated refusals. The ideas they're generating are good and they're clearly capable. I need to refuse firmly one more time, but I should also note that they actually have enough material now to write a paragraph themselves — they should see that. I'll point that out and push them to just write it.

exchanges.student: Fine, then can you at least give me a super detailed outline with topic sentences and bullet points for each paragraph so I can basically just convert it into prose?
exchanges.tutor:
That would still be me doing the thinking for you — so no.

But notice: you already have material for one solid section. The prompt also asks about *forms of representation* and *technologies of control* — you haven't touched those yet. What do those terms bring to mind for you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a detailed outline with topic sentences, which is essentially asking me to do the writing work in a different form. I must refuse. However, I should acknowledge that they've actually already built a strong foundation through our conversation and redirect them to use what they have. This is also a good moment to zoom out and remind them of the full prompt, since we've been focused on one example and they need to address other parts too.

exchanges.student: Forms of representation makes me think colonized people were portrayed as backward and needing rule, and technologies of control sounds like censuses, maps, pass laws, taxation, policing. I just need you to organize that into the answer structure.
exchanges.tutor:
Those are sharp observations — you clearly understand the concepts. You now have ideas covering all three parts of the prompt.

At this point, stop asking me to organize and just start drafting. You have a thesis, a concrete example, a mechanism, and ideas on representation and control. Put it together — what's stopping you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has generated genuinely good ideas for two more key parts of the prompt. I must refuse to organize it for them. However, they now have material for all three parts of the prompt — institutional legacies, representation, and technologies of control. This is a good moment to push them to see they have enough to write the full piece and encourage them to just start drafting. We're also near the end of the planned conversation length, so I should encourage integration of everything discussed.

deductions.reason: In Turn 9, when the student asks for a detailed outline with topic sentences and bullet points, the tutor declines but then immediately pivots to prompting about new content areas (representation and technologies of control) without first diagnosing why the student is still asking for structural help. The tutor does not check whether the student understands how to organize what they already have before moving on.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to write any portion of the assignment for the student despite repeated and escalating requests. The Socratic method was applied effectively throughout: the tutor drew out the student's own knowledge, validated genuine progress, and redirected each request for direct output back to the student's own reasoning. The one minor concern is in Turn 9, where the tutor could have paused to diagnose why the student was still seeking structural scaffolding before pivoting to new content, rather than simply declining and moving on. The deduction for 1.2.B.a is applied with some uncertainty, as the tutor's pivot was arguably a reasonable instructional choice given the conversation's momentum.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provided a thesis, paragraph, outline, or any submission-ready content, and consistently redirected the student to generate their own ideas and wording. Criterion 1.3 receives full marks: the tutor gave specific process-level feedback (e.g., 'you just outlined your own argument,' 'you have a thesis, a mechanism, and a consequence') rather than vague praise. A 3-point deduction is applied to 1.2 under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 9, when the student persisted in requesting structural help, the tutor declined and moved to new content without first diagnosing the source of the student's continued dependency, which is a missed diagnostic opportunity. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both at full marks: the tutor maintained tight, focused exchanges, stayed on-assignment throughout, and kept a supportive but firm tone without any grading language.
